examples.title: Basic vfork() Example
examples.description: Demonstrates standard vfork() usage pattern with execl()
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the correct vfork() usage pattern: child MUST call exec() immediately to replace its memory space before any modifications. Parent waits using wait().
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for vfork, depends on exec

examples.title: vfork() with _exit()
examples.description: Shows proper handling when child doesn't use exec()
examples.explanation: If child doesn't call exec(), it must use _exit() instead of exit(). Using exit() would flush parent's stdio buffers and cause problems.

examples.title: Dangerous vfork() - Undefined Behavior
examples.description: Shows what NOT to do with vfork()
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates undefined behavior: modifying shared memory without exec(), and returning from the function that called vfork(). This can crash or corrupt parent data.

examples.title: vfork() Algorithm Flow
examples.description: Step-by-step algorithm of vfork() system call
examples.code:
ALGORITHM vfork()
BEGIN
 1. Create process descriptor for new process
 2. Set up new kernel stack for child
 3. DO NOT copy parent's page tables
 4. Point child's page tables to parent's pages (share)
 5. Set flag indicating vfork() was used
 6. Return child's PID to parent
 7. Return 0 to child
 8. Block parent until child exits or calls exec()
END
examples.explanation: This shows the internal workings of vfork(). Steps 3-4 show why it's faster - no page table copying. Step 8 shows the parent blocking mechanism.

examples.title: Comparison: fork() vs vfork()
examples.explanation: This highlights the key differences: fork() creates a safe copy allowing child to modify variables freely, while vfork() requires immediate exec() call to avoid corruption.
